# **Day 02 \u2014 Time & Space Complexity + STL Algorithms (README.rst)**  
*30 Days to DSA in C++ \u2014 Ultra\u2011Premium Edition*

---

## **1. Introduction**

Time and space complexity form the mathematical backbone of algorithm analysis.  
Understanding them deeply allows you to:

- Predict performance  
- Optimize solutions  
- Choose the right data structure  
- Impress interviewers with clarity and precision  
- Avoid brute\u2011force traps  

Today, you will master:

- Big\u2011O notation  
- Best / average / worst\u2011case analysis  
- Space complexity patterns  
- Recurrence relations (intro)  
- STL algorithm complexities  
- Practical performance comparisons  
- Real interview examples  

This day sets the tone for the rest of the 30\u2011day journey.

---

## **2. Big\u2011O Notation \u2014 The Language of Performance**

Big\u2011O describes the **upper bound** of an algorithm\u2019s growth rate.

### **Common Complexities (From Fastest to Slowest)**

| Complexity | Example |
|-----------|---------|
| O(1) | Accessing array index |
| O(log n) | Binary search |
| O(n) | Linear scan |
| O(n log n) | Merge sort, quicksort avg |
| O(n\u00b2) | Nested loops |
| O(n\u00b3) | Triple nested loops |
| O(2\u207f) | Subset generation |
| O(n!) | Permutations |

---

## **3. Visual Intuition**

Growth comparison:

```
n:     1   2   4    8    16    32
logn:  0   1   2    3     4     5
n:     1   2   4    8    16    32
nlogn: 0   2   8   24    64   160
n^2:   1   4  16   64   256   1024
```

Notice how **n log n** grows much slower than **n\u00b2**.

---

## **4. Time Complexity Patterns**

### **4.1 Single Loop \u2192 O(n)**

```cpp
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { }
```

---

### **4.2 Nested Loops \u2192 O(n\u00b2)**

```cpp
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) { }
```

---

### **4.3 Logarithmic Loop \u2192 O(log n)**

```cpp
while (n > 1) n /= 2;
```

---

### **4.4 Two Pointers \u2192 O(n)**

```cpp
int i = 0, j = n - 1;
while (i < j) {
    i++;
    j--;
}
```

---

### **4.5 Binary Search \u2192 O(log n)**

```cpp
int l = 0, r = n - 1;
while (l <= r) {
    int mid = (l + r) / 2;
}
```

---

### **4.6 Recursion (Intro)**

Example:  
```
T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 \u2192 O(log n)
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \u2192 O(n log n)
```

---

## **5. Space Complexity**

Space complexity measures **extra memory** used.

### **Common Patterns**

| Pattern | Space |
|---------|--------|
| Variables | O(1) |
| Array of size n | O(n) |
| Recursion depth | O(n) worst |
| 2D matrix | O(n\u00b2) |

---

## **6. STL Algorithms \u2014 Complexity & Usage**

This is where Day 02 becomes extremely practical.

---

# **6.1 sort() \u2014 O(n log n)**

```cpp
sort(v.begin(), v.end());
```

### **Custom comparator**
```cpp
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](int a, int b){
    return a > b;
});
```

---

# **6.2 binary_search() \u2014 O(log n)**

```cpp
bool found = binary_search(v.begin(), v.end(), key);
```

---

# **6.3 lower_bound() / upper_bound() \u2014 O(log n)**

```cpp
auto it = lower_bound(v.begin(), v.end(), x);
```

---

# **6.4 reverse() \u2014 O(n)**

```cpp
reverse(v.begin(), v.end());
```

---

# **6.5 accumulate() \u2014 O(n)**

```cpp
int sum = accumulate(v.begin(), v.end(), 0);
```

---

# **6.6 count() \u2014 O(n)**

```cpp
int c = count(v.begin(), v.end(), 5);
```

---

# **6.7 find() \u2014 O(n)**

```cpp
auto it = find(v.begin(), v.end(), 10);
```

---

# **6.8 max_element() / min_element() \u2014 O(n)**

```cpp
int mx = *max_element(v.begin(), v.end());
```

---

# **6.9 next_permutation() \u2014 O(n)**

```cpp
next_permutation(v.begin(), v.end());
```

---

## **7. Real\u2011World Examples**

---

### **Example 1 \u2014 Count frequency using STL**

```cpp
unordered_map<int,int> freq;

for (int x : v)
    freq[x]++;
```

Time: O(n)  
Space: O(n)

---

### **Example 2 \u2014 Find first element greater than X**

```cpp
auto it = upper_bound(v.begin(), v.end(), x);
```

Time: O(log n)

---

### **Example 3 \u2014 Sort strings by length**

```cpp
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](string &a, string &b){
    return a.size() < b.size();
});
```

---

### **Example 4 \u2014 Check if array is sorted**

```cpp
bool sorted = is_sorted(v.begin(), v.end());
```

---

## **8. Interview\u2011Level Complexity Questions**

---

### **Q1: What is the complexity of inserting into a vector?**

- Amortized O(1) at end  
- O(n) at middle  

---

### **Q2: Complexity of unordered_map operations?**

- Average: O(1)  
- Worst: O(n) (rare)

---

### **Q3: Why is binary search O(log n)?**

Because each step halves the search space.

---

### **Q4: Why is merge sort O(n log n)?**

Because:

- log n levels  
- n work per level  

---

## **9. Exercises (Try First)**

---

### **Exercise 1**  
Given a vector of size n, count how many elements are strictly greater than the average.

---

### **Exercise 2**  
Given a sorted vector, find the first and last occurrence of a number using STL.

---

### **Exercise 3**  
Given an array, compute prefix sums using accumulate.

---

### **Exercise 4**  
Given a vector of pairs, sort by second value descending, and if equal, by first ascending.

---

for (int i = 1; i < n; i *= 2)
    for (int j = 0; j < i; j++)

# **Day 2 \u2013 THEORY.rst**  
## **\u23f1\ufe0f Time & Space Complexity + \u2699\ufe0f STL Algorithms Mastery**

---

# **\ud83c\udf1f 1. Overview**

Day 2 builds the mathematical backbone of your DSA journey:

- \u23f1\ufe0f **Time Complexity**  
- \ud83d\udcbe **Space Complexity**  
- \u2699\ufe0f **STL Algorithms**  
- \ud83d\udcca **Big\u2011O intuition**  
- \ud83e\udde0 **How to analyze code efficiently**  

This chapter makes you fluent in understanding **how fast** and **how memory\u2011efficient** your programs are \u2014 a skill every top\u2011tier engineer must master.

---

# **\ud83d\udcd8 2. Big\u2011O Notation (The Language of Complexity)**

Big\u2011O describes **how performance scales** with input size `n`.

---

## **\ud83d\udfe6 2.1 Common Time Complexities (Memorize This Table)**

| Complexity | Name | Example |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **O(1)** | Constant | Accessing array index |
| **O(log n)** | Logarithmic | Binary search |
| **O(n)** | Linear | Loop through array |
| **O(n log n)** | Log\u2011linear | Merge sort, sort() |
| **O(n\u00b2)** | Quadratic | Nested loops |
| **O(2\u207f)** | Exponential | Subset generation |
| **O(n!)** | Factorial | Permutations |

---

## **\ud83d\udfe9 2.2 Visual Intuition (Fast \u2192 Slow)**

```
O(1)  \u2192  O(log n)  \u2192  O(n)  \u2192  O(n log n)  \u2192  O(n\u00b2)  \u2192  O(2\u207f)  \u2192  O(n!)
```

Think of it like **speed tiers**:

- \ud83d\ude80 O(1), O(log n) \u2192 blazing fast  
- \u26a1 O(n), O(n log n) \u2192 acceptable  
- \ud83d\udc22 O(n\u00b2) \u2192 slow for large n  
- \ud83d\udc0c O(2\u207f), O(n!) \u2192 avoid unless n \u2264 20  

---

# **\ud83e\uddee 3. Time Complexity \u2013 Deep Dive**

---

## **\ud83d\udfe6 3.1 O(1) \u2013 Constant Time**

Operations that don\u2019t depend on input size:

- Accessing `arr[i]`  
- Pushing to `vector` (amortized)  
- Hash map insert/search (average)  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe9 3.2 O(log n) \u2013 Logarithmic**

Divide\u2011and\u2011conquer operations:

- Binary search  
- Heap operations  
- Balanced BST operations  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe8 3.3 O(n) \u2013 Linear**

Single loop:

```
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
```

---

## **\ud83d\udfe7 3.4 O(n log n) \u2013 Log\u2011Linear**

Sorting algorithms:

- `sort()`  
- Merge sort  
- Heap sort  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe5 3.5 O(n\u00b2) \u2013 Quadratic**

Nested loops:

```
for i:
  for j:
```

Used in:

- Matrix operations  
- Brute force pair checking  

---

## **\ud83d\udfea 3.6 O(2\u207f) \u2013 Exponential**

Used in:

- Subsets  
- Recursion without memoization  

---

## **\ud83d\udfeb 3.7 O(n!) \u2013 Factorial**

Used in:

- Permutations  
- Traveling Salesman brute force  

---

# **\ud83d\udcbe 4. Space Complexity**

Measures **extra memory** used.

---

## **\ud83d\udfe6 4.1 O(1) \u2013 Constant Space**

- Two pointers  
- In\u2011place algorithms  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe9 4.2 O(n) \u2013 Linear Space**

- Storing arrays  
- Recursion stack  
- Hash maps  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe8 4.3 O(n\u00b2) \u2013 Quadratic Space**

- 2D matrices  
- DP tables  

---

# **\u2699\ufe0f 5. STL Algorithms \u2013 The Powerhouse**

STL algorithms are **highly optimized** and should be your default choice.

---

## **\ud83d\udfe6 5.1 Sorting Algorithms**

### **sort() \u2013 O(n log n)**  
Uses **Introsort** (QuickSort + HeapSort + MergeSort hybrid).

```
sort(v.begin(), v.end());
```

### **Custom comparator**
```
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](int a, int b){
    return a > b;
});
```

---

## **\ud83d\udfe9 5.2 Searching Algorithms**

### **binary_search() \u2013 O(log n)**  
Checks if element exists.

### **lower_bound() \u2013 O(log n)**  
First element \u2265 x.

### **upper_bound() \u2013 O(log n)**  
First element > x.

---

## **\ud83d\udfe8 5.3 Useful STL Algorithms**

| Algorithm | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `reverse()` | Reverse container |
| `max_element()` | Largest element |
| `min_element()` | Smallest element |
| `accumulate()` | Sum of elements |
| `count()` | Count occurrences |
| `unique()` | Remove duplicates |
| `erase()` | Remove elements |

---

# **\ud83e\udde0 6. How to Analyze Code Quickly**

---

## **\ud83d\udfe6 6.1 Rule 1 \u2014 Count Loops**

- One loop \u2192 O(n)  
- Nested loops \u2192 O(n\u00b2)  
- Loop inside binary search \u2192 O(n log n)  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe9 6.2 Rule 2 \u2014 Look for Recursion**

- Recursion without memo \u2192 exponential  
- Recursion with memo \u2192 polynomial  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe8 6.3 Rule 3 \u2014 Check STL Operations**

- `sort()` \u2192 O(n log n)  
- `push_back()` \u2192 O(1) amortized  
- `map` insert \u2192 O(log n)  
- `unordered_map` insert \u2192 O(1) average  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe7 6.4 Rule 4 \u2014 Ignore Constants**

```
O(3n + 5) \u2192 O(n)
```

---

# **\ud83d\udcda 7. Common Patterns (Must Memorize)**

---

## **\ud83d\udfe6 Pattern 1 \u2014 Two Pointers**

Used in:

- Pair sum  
- Removing duplicates  
- Sliding window  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe9 Pattern 2 \u2014 Binary Search on Sorted Data**

Used in:

- Searching  
- Optimization problems  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe8 Pattern 3 \u2014 Hash Map for Frequency**

Used in:

- Anagrams  
- Subarray sum  
- Hashing problems  

---

## **\ud83d\udfe7 Pattern 4 \u2014 Sorting + Greedy**

Used in:

- Activity selection  
- Interval scheduling  

---

# **\ud83c\udfaf 8. Interview\u2011Level Insights**

- Always prefer STL algorithms over manual loops  
- Use `unordered_map` for O(1) average lookups  
- Use `vector` unless you specifically need linked list behavior  
- Understand time complexity of each STL container  
- Avoid nested loops unless necessary  

---

# **\ud83c\udfc1 9. Conclusion**

Day 2 gives you the **mathematical intuition** behind algorithm performance and the **STL algorithm toolkit** to write efficient code.

You now understand:

- Time complexity  
- Space complexity  
- STL algorithms  
- How to analyze code  
- How to choose the right container  

This foundation will be used **every single day** going forward.
